entry_requirements.asvab_line_score: OF (Operators and Food) composite ≥ 85, per two independent secondary sources (operationmilitarykids.org, part-time-commander.com), consistent with the Army:OF composite listed for this cluster in data/interest-taxonomy.json. UNVERIFIED at primary-source level — the official goarmy.com 92G page did not render score data on direct fetch this session (JavaScript-rendered page); confirm the current cutoff with a recruiter.
entry_requirements.education: High school diploma or GED (Army general accession standard; no 92G-specific deviation found).
entry_requirements.age_range: 17 (with parental consent) to 42, per the Army's 2026 policy ceiling (raised from 35) — data/branch-profiles.json. No 92G-specific age restriction found.
entry_requirements.physical: Standard Army accession medical and fitness standards; no elevated physical/vision standard specific to 92G was found. Must pass the Army Fitness Test (AFT) like all soldiers.

training_pipeline.location: One of four Army training centers (Fort Jackson SC, Fort Moore GA, Fort Sill OK, or Fort Leonard Wood MO)
training_pipeline.description: Standard Army basic training common to all MOSs. (data/branch-profiles.json)

training_pipeline.stage: Advanced Individual Training (AIT) — Culinary Specialist Course
training_pipeline.location: Joint Culinary Center of Excellence, Fort Gregg-Adams, VA (formerly Fort Lee; renamed 2023)
training_pipeline.description: Baking, small- and large-scale garrison food service, and field feeding using the Mobile Kitchen Trailer (MKT) or Containerized Kitchen (CK). Length reported as 9 weeks by one source and 8 weeks 2 days by another — reported here as 9 (rounded), flagged as not independently confirmed against a primary Army source this session.

pay_and_compensation.methodology_note: Computed, not authoritative: (E-1, <2 YOS basic pay $2,407.20/mo + enlisted BAS $476.95/mo) x 12 = $34,609.80/yr. BAH is intentionally EXCLUDED because it is duty-station-specific and cannot be reduced to one national number (pay-tables.json bah.national_number_warning) — actual total cash compensation is materially higher once a real BAH figure for the member's duty station is added, often the single largest component. Excludes the DoD TSP contribution (up to 5% of base pay, ~$1,444/yr at max match) and TRICARE's non-cash value, neither of which pay-tables.json could quantify to primary-source confidence this session.
pay_and_compensation.source: data/pay-tables.json (RUN COMPENSATION, this project), citing military.com/benefits/military-pay/charts and navycs.com/charts/2026-military-pay-chart.html (primary DFAS/militarypay.defense.gov pages returned HTTP 403 on direct fetch)

bonuses.conditions: No current, verifiable culinary/food-service enlistment bonus was found for any branch this session. Culinary specialties are historically LOW-bonus or NO-bonus MOSs/ratings across the services — they are consistently low-ASVAB-barrier, high-accession, low-shortage fields, which is the opposite profile of the technical/shortage specialties that draw large bonuses. A web-search summary surfaced a 'Ranger Bonus' figure ostensibly tied to Army 92G from the HRC bonus chart; this reads as a search-summarizer artifact (a Ranger bonus has no logical connection to a culinary MOS) and was NOT used. A direct fetch of the official HRC bonus chart PDF (hrc.army.mil, effective 2025-09-26) failed to parse in this session's tooling, so 92G's presence or absence on that list could not be independently confirmed either way. Bonuses are set quarterly per branch manning needs and change without notice — verify current 92G/CS/3F1X1/3381 bonus eligibility and amount directly with a recruiter before relying on any number.
bonuses.source: HRC Bonus Chart effective 2025-09-26 (hrc.army.mil — fetched but unparseable this session); general bonus-structure knowledge from data/retirement.json continuation-pay research (this project)

career_progression.typical_promotion_timeline: E-1→E-4 in roughly 2-3 years is the Army-wide norm; E-5 (Sergeant) board eligibility typically follows at ~4-6 years of service. No 92G-specific timeline deviation was found — treat as the general Army enlisted pattern rather than a sourced 92G-specific figure.
reenlistment_decision_points: End of initial term of service (commonly a 3- or 4-year first contract)
Mid-career (~8-10 years) reenlistment tied to promotion/NCO development school completion
The 20-year retention decision (pension cliff — see retirement block)
advanced_schools_or_certs: Certified Culinary Educator (CCE) — listed against 92G on Army COOL (cool.osd.mil)
Certified Chef de Cuisine (CCC), Certified Executive Chef (CEC), Certified Master Chef (CMC) — ACF certifications listed against 92G on Army COOL, gated by documented experience hours, not automatic
Basic/Advanced NCO courses at each promotion gate (standard Army NCOES, not 92G-specific)
career_progression.source: cool.osd.mil/army/moc/index.html?moc=92g (certification listings; fetch attempt failed with a certificate error this session, corroborated via search snippet only — UNVERIFIED at primary-fetch level)

retirement.twenty_year_cliff_note: The pension is all-or-nothing at 20 years — there is no partial/prorated defined-benefit pension for separating at 19 years or 10 years. Before 20 years, a culinary specialist keeps every dollar of their own TSP contributions and earnings (always vested), plus DoD's automatic 1%-of-pay TSP contribution and its match (vested after 2 years of service) — all portable into a civilian 401(k)/IRA. The defined-benefit pension itself is 'entirely forfeited' below 20 years, per DoD's own language. This applies identically regardless of specialty; culinary carries no special rule.
retirement.continuation_pay_note: One-time cash payout available between roughly year 7 and year 12 of service (exact window and multiplier set annually per branch; Army's published active-component rate is 2.5x monthly basic pay for a 4-year additional service obligation). No culinary-specific continuation-pay rate was identified this session — treat the general BRS figure as the baseline and verify current-branch amount before relying on it.
retirement.guard_reserve_points_note: Guard/Reserve retirement is NOT interchangeable with active-duty retirement. It runs on a points system (1 point/drill, 1 point/AD day, up to 15 membership points/year) toward a 20-'good year' qualifying threshold, with a non-regular pension that generally starts at age 60 (reducible, not below age 50, for qualifying post-2008 mobilized duty). Applies identically to Guard/Reserve culinary billets as to any other specialty.
retirement.lump_sum_option_note: At 20+ years, a retiring member may elect 25% or 50% of the discounted present value of future retired pay as an immediate lump sum, in exchange for reduced monthly retired pay until full Social Security retirement age (~67), when the monthly amount returns to the uncut level. Fully taxable in the year received.

transition.skillbridge_notes: DoD-wide SkillBridge eligibility (up to 180 days of permissive duty in the final window before separation) is not specialty-restricted, so culinary specialists near separation are as eligible as any other MOS/rating in principle — subject to the same real gate as everyone else: unit commander approval, which is discretionary based on unit staffing/operational needs (data/transition.json). Whether hospitality/food-service industry partners (e.g., hotel or restaurant-group SkillBridge programs) are commonly available in a given service member's location was not independently verified this session — flagged as an inference, not a sourced claim.
transition.tap_program_summary: Mandatory DoD-wide Transition Assistance Program: pre-separation counseling, a VA Benefits I course, a DOL employment-fundamentals workshop, and an optional 2-day track (Higher Education, Career/Technical Training, or Entrepreneurship/Boots to Business), ending in a Capstone that certifies Career Readiness Standards are met. Must begin no later than 365 days before separation. Applies identically regardless of specialty.
transition.dd214_significance: The DD-214's 'character of service' entry gates eligibility for nearly every benefit below — VA disability, VA healthcare, GI Bill, VA home loan, and most state veteran benefits. No culinary-specific significance beyond the general rule.
va_disability_common_claims: GUESS/INFERENCE, not sourced from a specialty-linked VA claims dataset this session (data/transition.json's specialty_linked_common_claims list covers combat arms, aviation, infantry, and PACT Act-covered theater deployment, but does NOT include a food-service/culinary entry). Reasoned from the physical nature of the job rather than pulled from a VA claims-frequency source:
Musculoskeletal conditions (lower back, knees, plantar fasciitis) from years of prolonged standing and repetitive lifting of large-quantity cookware/supplies in a galley or dining facility
Burns, scars, and lacerations from high-volume/high-heat kitchen equipment

Hearing loss/tinnitus for shipboard and submarine galley duty (proximity to engineering spaces, ventilation, and dish-machine noise) — plausible by analogy to the aviation/combat-arms noise-exposure claims pattern in data/transition.json, but not independently confirmed for culinary specifically
To verify: check VA's published claims-by-occupation or claims-by-MOS data (if VA publishes it) or consult a VSO familiar with food-service ratings before treating this list as authoritative.
transition.vr_e_eligibility_note: Requires a 20%+ service-connected disability rating creating an employment handicap, filed within a 12-year window from separation or first rating. Funds up to 48 months of training/education plus a monthly subsistence allowance (FY2026 max ~$3,439.23/mo per data/transition.json), drawn from a separate entitlement bucket than the Post-9/11 GI Bill. No culinary-specific eligibility variance.

relevant_certifications: ACF Certified Culinarian (CC) through Certified Executive Chef (CEC) — scaled by verified experience hours, not automatic
ServSafe Food Handler / Food Protection Manager certification
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: This is the most consequential honest fact about this cluster: military culinary service does NOT confer an ACF certification automatically. ACF requires a Joint Service Transcript, a work-verification memorandum documenting actual verified hands-on hours (not just training time), and supplemental Food Safety/Nutrition certs before it will grant a certification level — and even the entry-level Certified Culinarian credential requires meeting ACF's documented-experience threshold. Civilian food-service pay is also genuinely modest relative to many other military crosswalks: BLS's May 2024 OES data puts the median for Cooks, Institution and Cafeteria at $38,810/yr and Chefs and Head Cooks (a more senior, harder-to-reach role) at $60,990/yr — this is one of the lower-paying civilian crosswalks in this project, and should be represented honestly rather than dressed up.
civilian_crosswalk.source: https://www.bls.gov/oes/2023/may/oes352012.htm and https://www.bls.gov/oes/2023/may/oes351011.htm (BLS OEWS, May 2024 reference period, figures via web search of the May 2025 OEWS release); https://www.acfchefs.org/ACF/Certify/MilitaryCertification/ACF/Certify/Military/

veteran_benefits.gi_bill_tier_note: Post-9/11 GI Bill (Ch. 33) benefit tier scales with cumulative qualifying active-duty days: 100% at 36+ months (or Purple Heart / service-connected-disability discharge after 30+ days), down through 90%, 80%, 70%, 60%, to a 50% floor at 90 days-5 months. No tier below 50%. Only qualifying Title 10 active-duty days count — drills, AT, and initial training duty status do not. Applies identically regardless of specialty.
veteran_benefits.gi_bill_transferability_note: Transferable to a spouse and/or dependent children (up to 36 months combined) after 6 years of service, in exchange for a 4-year additional service-obligation commitment — a real service commitment, not paperwork-only. Must be requested via milConnect while still serving; cannot be initiated after separation. Dependent children must wait until the member has 10+ years of service to start using it.
veteran_benefits.va_home_loan_note: VA-backed home loans generally require no down payment and no monthly PMI for veterans with full entitlement, subject to lender underwriting (credit/income/DTI) and a funding fee (1.25%-3.3% of loan amount depending on down payment and first/subsequent use, waived for veterans drawing service-connected disability compensation).
veteran_benefits.va_healthcare_priority_group_note: VA assigns one of 8 priority groups at enrollment based on service-connected disability rating, combat/toxic-exposure service history, income, and other VA-benefit receipt — not by specialty. A culinary specialist's group assignment depends entirely on their individual disability rating and income profile, not their MOS/rating.
veteran_benefits.state_benefit_variance_note: State veteran benefits (property-tax exemptions, income-tax treatment, tuition waivers, etc.) are set by individual state legislatures and are NOT a single national figure — e.g., a 100%-disabled veteran gets a full residence-homestead property-tax exemption in Texas but a flat $100,000 assessed-value exemption in California (data/veteran-benefits.json). Verify against the veteran's specific state of legal residence.

education_benefits.gi_bill_summary: Post-9/11 GI Bill pays full tuition/fees at public schools (in-state rate applies to all users) or up to $29,920.95/yr (2025-2026 AY) at private/foreign schools, plus a housing allowance keyed to the school's zip code and a $1,000/yr book stipend — scaled by the eligibility tier above. (data/education-benefits.json)
education_benefits.tuition_assistance: DoD-wide Tuition Assistance: $250/semester-hour, capped at $4,500/fiscal year, while still serving. Applies the same across all six branches per data/education-benefits.json; branch-specific semester-hour usage limits were not independently confirmed to primary-source level in that file.
education_benefits.credentialing_assistance: Army Credentialing Assistance (CA)/Army COOL: reduced to a $2,000/fiscal-year cap in 2024, lifetime limit reported at 3 credentials per 10 years of service; commissioned officers reported ineligible. UNVERIFIED at primary-source level — cool.osd.mil could not be independently fetched this session (certificate error). (data/education-benefits.json)
education_benefits.coop_or_apprenticeship_notes: American Culinary Federation (ACF) certification is the relevant civilian credential pathway (Certified Culinarian through Certified Executive Chef, scaled by verified experience hours) — it is NOT automatic from military service. Applicants submit a Joint Service Transcript, civilian transcripts if any, a work-verification memorandum documenting actual hands-on hours, and supplemental Food Safety/Nutrition certifications; ACF then determines which certification level the documented military experience qualifies for. (acfchefs.org U.S. Military Certification Program page, cited via search synthesis this session; Army COOL's ACF Certification Checklist PDF at quartermaster.army.mil corroborates the JST + work-verification requirement.) Coast Guard specifically offers Coast Guard-funded pathways to an associate or bachelor's degree in Culinary Arts for CS personnel (gocoastguard.com/careers/enlisted/cs, fetched 2026-07-12).

deployment_and_lifestyle.typical_tempo: Deploys with the supported unit to run field feeding (Mobile Kitchen Trailer/Containerized Kitchen) during exercises and deployments, and staffs garrison dining facilities between deployments. No sourced deployment-frequency figure specific to 92G was found this session; tempo generally follows the supported unit's operational tempo rather than a rating-specific rotation.
deployment_and_lifestyle.family_considerations: Garrison assignments are relatively family-stable compared to sea-service culinary billets (no underway rotations); deployments follow the supported unit's schedule, which varies widely by unit type.

entry_requirements.asvab_line_score: UNVERIFIED — no specific numeric minimum located this session. data/interest-taxonomy.json lists MAGE:G (the Air Force General aptitude composite) as the relevant composite for this cluster, but the current numeric cutoff for 3F1X1 was not confirmed (the official CFETP PDF at static.e-publishing.af.mil returned HTTP 403 on direct fetch this session). Confirm with a recruiter or MyVECTOR.
entry_requirements.education: High school diploma or GED (Air Force general accession standard; no 3F1X1-specific deviation found).
entry_requirements.age_range: 17 (with parental consent) to 42, per data/branch-profiles.json (secondary-reported 2023 increase from 39, not independently confirmed on airforce.com this session).
entry_requirements.physical: Standard Air Force accession medical and fitness standards; no 3F1X1-specific elevated standard found.
entry_requirements.other: IMPORTANT SCOPE NOTE: 3F1X1 ("Services") is a broader force-support career field, not a culinary-only specialty — it also covers lodging operations, fitness/recreation programs, and mortuary affairs, per the official CFETP title and multiple corroborating secondary sources (foreverwingman.com, operationmilitarykids.org, mosroadmap.com). Airmen in this AFSC rotate through food-service duty as one of several functional areas rather than specializing in culinary work exclusively the way Army 92G, Navy CS, USMC 3381, or Coast Guard CS do. CORRECTION TO PROJECT TAXONOMY: data/interest-taxonomy.json currently lists the Air Force code for this cluster as '3F0X2 — Services'; this session's research consistently found the current designation to be 3F1X1 (confirmed via the official CFETP document title 'AFSC 3F1X1 SERVICES' at static.e-publishing.af.mil, and cross-corroborated by 4 independent secondary sources). Recommend the taxonomy file be corrected from 3F0X2 to 3F1X1 in a future pass; flagged here rather than silently overridden.

training_pipeline.location: Joint Base San Antonio-Lackland (JBSA-Lackland), San Antonio, TX
training_pipeline.description: Commonly described as ~7.5 weeks; recorded as 7 because this schema field is an integer. (data/branch-profiles.json)

training_pipeline.location: Joint Culinary Center of Excellence, Fort Gregg-Adams, VA (co-located with Army/Navy culinary training)
training_pipeline.description: One search result described food-service-specific technical training as '29 days' at Fort Lee (now Fort Gregg-Adams); reported here as 4 weeks (rounded). This is for the food-service portion specifically — the broader 3F1X1 AFSC's full technical-training curriculum (covering lodging, fitness/recreation, and mortuary-affairs functions as well) was not independently confirmed this session; the official CFETP PDF that would settle this returned HTTP 403 on direct fetch. UNVERIFIED at primary-source level.

pay_and_compensation.methodology_note: Computed, not authoritative: (E-1, <2 YOS basic pay $2,407.20/mo + enlisted BAS $476.95/mo) x 12 = $34,609.80/yr. BAH is intentionally EXCLUDED because it is duty-station-specific and cannot be reduced to one national number (pay-tables.json bah.national_number_warning) — actual total cash compensation is materially higher once a real BAH figure for the member's duty station is added, often the single largest component. Excludes the DoD TSP contribution (up to 5% of base pay, ~$1,444/yr at max match) and TRICARE's non-cash value, neither of which pay-tables.json could quantify to primary-source confidence this session.
pay_and_compensation.source: data/pay-tables.json (RUN COMPENSATION, this project), citing military.com/benefits/military-pay/charts and navycs.com/charts/2026-military-pay-chart.html (primary DFAS/militarypay.defense.gov pages returned HTTP 403 on direct fetch)

bonuses.conditions: No current, verifiable culinary/food-service enlistment bonus was found for any branch this session. Culinary specialties are historically LOW-bonus or NO-bonus MOSs/ratings across the services — they are consistently low-ASVAB-barrier, high-accession, low-shortage fields, which is the opposite profile of the technical/shortage specialties that draw large bonuses. A web-search summary surfaced a 'Ranger Bonus' figure ostensibly tied to Army 92G from the HRC bonus chart; this reads as a search-summarizer artifact (a Ranger bonus has no logical connection to a culinary MOS) and was NOT used. A direct fetch of the official HRC bonus chart PDF (hrc.army.mil, effective 2025-09-26) failed to parse in this session's tooling, so 92G's presence or absence on that list could not be independently confirmed either way. Bonuses are set quarterly per branch manning needs and change without notice — verify current 92G/CS/3F1X1/3381 bonus eligibility and amount directly with a recruiter before relying on any number.
bonuses.source: HRC Bonus Chart effective 2025-09-26 (hrc.army.mil — fetched but unparseable this session); general bonus-structure knowledge from data/retirement.json continuation-pay research (this project)

career_progression.typical_promotion_timeline: General Air Force enlisted promotion pattern (E-1→E-4 in ~2-3 years, E-5 by ~5 years via the Weighted Airman Promotion System); no 3F1X1-specific deviation found this session.
reenlistment_decision_points: End of initial enlistment (commonly 4 or 6 years)
Cross-functional rotation among Services sub-areas (food service, lodging, fitness/recreation, mortuary affairs) at each assignment
The 20-year retention decision (pension cliff — see retirement block)
advanced_schools_or_certs: AFSC skill-level upgrade training (3F131 apprentice → 3F151 journeyman → 3F171 craftsman, per career-progression pattern referenced in search results)
ACF certifications available via Air Force Credentialing Assistance, though specific 3F1X1-linked credential listings were not independently confirmed this session

retirement.twenty_year_cliff_note: The pension is all-or-nothing at 20 years — there is no partial/prorated defined-benefit pension for separating at 19 years or 10 years. Before 20 years, a culinary specialist keeps every dollar of their own TSP contributions and earnings (always vested), plus DoD's automatic 1%-of-pay TSP contribution and its match (vested after 2 years of service) — all portable into a civilian 401(k)/IRA. The defined-benefit pension itself is 'entirely forfeited' below 20 years, per DoD's own language. This applies identically regardless of specialty; culinary carries no special rule.
retirement.continuation_pay_note: One-time cash payout available between roughly year 7 and year 12 of service (exact window and multiplier set annually per branch; Army's published active-component rate is 2.5x monthly basic pay for a 4-year additional service obligation). No culinary-specific continuation-pay rate was identified this session — treat the general BRS figure as the baseline and verify current-branch amount before relying on it.
retirement.guard_reserve_points_note: Guard/Reserve retirement is NOT interchangeable with active-duty retirement. It runs on a points system (1 point/drill, 1 point/AD day, up to 15 membership points/year) toward a 20-'good year' qualifying threshold, with a non-regular pension that generally starts at age 60 (reducible, not below age 50, for qualifying post-2008 mobilized duty). Applies identically to Guard/Reserve culinary billets as to any other specialty.
retirement.lump_sum_option_note: At 20+ years, a retiring member may elect 25% or 50% of the discounted present value of future retired pay as an immediate lump sum, in exchange for reduced monthly retired pay until full Social Security retirement age (~67), when the monthly amount returns to the uncut level. Fully taxable in the year received.

transition.skillbridge_notes: DoD-wide SkillBridge eligibility (up to 180 days of permissive duty in the final window before separation) is not specialty-restricted, so culinary specialists near separation are as eligible as any other MOS/rating in principle — subject to the same real gate as everyone else: unit commander approval, which is discretionary based on unit staffing/operational needs (data/transition.json). Whether hospitality/food-service industry partners (e.g., hotel or restaurant-group SkillBridge programs) are commonly available in a given service member's location was not independently verified this session — flagged as an inference, not a sourced claim.
transition.tap_program_summary: Mandatory DoD-wide Transition Assistance Program: pre-separation counseling, a VA Benefits I course, a DOL employment-fundamentals workshop, and an optional 2-day track (Higher Education, Career/Technical Training, or Entrepreneurship/Boots to Business), ending in a Capstone that certifies Career Readiness Standards are met. Must begin no later than 365 days before separation. Applies identically regardless of specialty.
transition.dd214_significance: The DD-214's 'character of service' entry gates eligibility for nearly every benefit below — VA disability, VA healthcare, GI Bill, VA home loan, and most state veteran benefits. No culinary-specific significance beyond the general rule.
va_disability_common_claims: GUESS/INFERENCE, not sourced from a specialty-linked VA claims dataset this session (data/transition.json's specialty_linked_common_claims list covers combat arms, aviation, infantry, and PACT Act-covered theater deployment, but does NOT include a food-service/culinary entry). Reasoned from the physical nature of the job rather than pulled from a VA claims-frequency source:
Musculoskeletal conditions (lower back, knees, plantar fasciitis) from years of prolonged standing and repetitive lifting of large-quantity cookware/supplies in a galley or dining facility
Burns, scars, and lacerations from high-volume/high-heat kitchen equipment

Hearing loss/tinnitus for shipboard and submarine galley duty (proximity to engineering spaces, ventilation, and dish-machine noise) — plausible by analogy to the aviation/combat-arms noise-exposure claims pattern in data/transition.json, but not independently confirmed for culinary specifically
To verify: check VA's published claims-by-occupation or claims-by-MOS data (if VA publishes it) or consult a VSO familiar with food-service ratings before treating this list as authoritative.
transition.vr_e_eligibility_note: Requires a 20%+ service-connected disability rating creating an employment handicap, filed within a 12-year window from separation or first rating. Funds up to 48 months of training/education plus a monthly subsistence allowance (FY2026 max ~$3,439.23/mo per data/transition.json), drawn from a separate entitlement bucket than the Post-9/11 GI Bill. No culinary-specific eligibility variance.

example_civilian_titles: Hospitality/lodging operations (reflecting this AFSC's broader scope beyond culinary alone)
relevant_certifications: ACF Certified Culinarian (CC) through Certified Executive Chef (CEC) — scaled by verified experience hours, not automatic
ServSafe Food Handler / Food Protection Manager certification
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: Same civilian-license caveat as the other culinary records: no automatic ACF certification. An ADDITIONAL, AFSC-specific gap: because 3F1X1 splits time across food service, lodging, fitness/recreation, and mortuary affairs, an individual airman's actual hands-on culinary hours (the documentation ACF requires) may be lower than a specialist who works food service exclusively (Army 92G, Navy CS, USMC 3381, Coast Guard CS) — worth flagging honestly to a candidate who wants maximum culinary-only experience.

veteran_benefits.gi_bill_tier_note: Post-9/11 GI Bill (Ch. 33) benefit tier scales with cumulative qualifying active-duty days: 100% at 36+ months (or Purple Heart / service-connected-disability discharge after 30+ days), down through 90%, 80%, 70%, 60%, to a 50% floor at 90 days-5 months. No tier below 50%. Only qualifying Title 10 active-duty days count — drills, AT, and initial training duty status do not. Applies identically regardless of specialty.
veteran_benefits.gi_bill_transferability_note: Transferable to a spouse and/or dependent children (up to 36 months combined) after 6 years of service, in exchange for a 4-year additional service-obligation commitment — a real service commitment, not paperwork-only. Must be requested via milConnect while still serving; cannot be initiated after separation. Dependent children must wait until the member has 10+ years of service to start using it.
veteran_benefits.va_home_loan_note: VA-backed home loans generally require no down payment and no monthly PMI for veterans with full entitlement, subject to lender underwriting (credit/income/DTI) and a funding fee (1.25%-3.3% of loan amount depending on down payment and first/subsequent use, waived for veterans drawing service-connected disability compensation).
veteran_benefits.va_healthcare_priority_group_note: VA assigns one of 8 priority groups at enrollment based on service-connected disability rating, combat/toxic-exposure service history, income, and other VA-benefit receipt — not by specialty. A culinary specialist's group assignment depends entirely on their individual disability rating and income profile, not their MOS/rating.
veteran_benefits.state_benefit_variance_note: State veteran benefits (property-tax exemptions, income-tax treatment, tuition waivers, etc.) are set by individual state legislatures and are NOT a single national figure — e.g., a 100%-disabled veteran gets a full residence-homestead property-tax exemption in Texas but a flat $100,000 assessed-value exemption in California (data/veteran-benefits.json). Verify against the veteran's specific state of legal residence.

education_benefits.gi_bill_summary: Post-9/11 GI Bill pays full tuition/fees at public schools (in-state rate applies to all users) or up to $29,920.95/yr (2025-2026 AY) at private/foreign schools, plus a housing allowance keyed to the school's zip code and a $1,000/yr book stipend — scaled by the eligibility tier above. (data/education-benefits.json)
education_benefits.tuition_assistance: DoD-wide Tuition Assistance: $250/semester-hour, capped at $4,500/fiscal year, while still serving. Applies the same across all six branches per data/education-benefits.json; branch-specific semester-hour usage limits were not independently confirmed to primary-source level in that file.
education_benefits.credentialing_assistance: Air Force Credentialing Assistance (via AFVEC): reported cap of up to $4,500/year. UNVERIFIED at primary-source level — cool.osd.mil could not be independently fetched this session. (data/education-benefits.json)
education_benefits.coop_or_apprenticeship_notes: American Culinary Federation (ACF) certification is the relevant civilian credential pathway (Certified Culinarian through Certified Executive Chef, scaled by verified experience hours) — it is NOT automatic from military service. Applicants submit a Joint Service Transcript, civilian transcripts if any, a work-verification memorandum documenting actual hands-on hours, and supplemental Food Safety/Nutrition certifications; ACF then determines which certification level the documented military experience qualifies for. (acfchefs.org U.S. Military Certification Program page, cited via search synthesis this session; Army COOL's ACF Certification Checklist PDF at quartermaster.army.mil corroborates the JST + work-verification requirement.) Coast Guard specifically offers Coast Guard-funded pathways to an associate or bachelor's degree in Culinary Arts for CS personnel (gocoastguard.com/careers/enlisted/cs, fetched 2026-07-12).

deployment_and_lifestyle.typical_tempo: Garrison-centered role supporting dining facilities on Air Force installations, with deployment tempo tied to the supported base/unit's Air Expeditionary Force rotation rather than a sea-service underway cycle. No 3F1X1-specific deployment-frequency figure was found this session.
deployment_and_lifestyle.family_considerations: Generally more family-stable than sea-service culinary billets given the installation-centered, non-seagoing nature of the work; the broader Services scope (lodging, fitness, mortuary affairs) may mean varied duty locations within an installation rather than a single kitchen assignment.

entry_requirements.asvab_line_score: UNVERIFIED — no rating-specific composite score located this session. Coast Guard uses summed raw subtest scores per rating (like the Navy) rather than a single published line score, consistent with data/interest-taxonomy.json's 'no line score — per-rating subtest sums' note for this cluster. The general Coast Guard AFQT floor is 32 with a high school diploma (50 with a GED), lowered from 40 in November 2023, but this is the general accession floor, not a CS-specific rating cutoff. Confirm the current CS-specific subtest requirement with a recruiter.
entry_requirements.education: High school diploma or GED (Coast Guard general accession standard; AFQT ≥ 32 with diploma per data/branch-profiles.json).
entry_requirements.age_range: 17 (with parental consent) up to 42, per a Coast Guard recruiting-instruction update (GAO-25-107224) that raised the prior ceiling of 34 — data/branch-profiles.json.

entry_requirements.other: NAMING NOTE: The Coast Guard renamed this rating from 'Food Service Specialist (FS)' to 'Culinary Specialist (CS)' on 2017-01-06, to better reflect the culinary skill level involved (per Wikipedia summary of official Coast Guard messaging, cross-referenced against gocoastguard.com/careers/enlisted/cs which uses 'CS' and 'Culinary Specialist' throughout). data/interest-taxonomy.json still lists this rating as 'FS — Food Service Specialist,' which is the pre-2017 name; recommend the taxonomy file be updated to 'CS — Culinary Specialist' in a future pass. Flagged here rather than silently corrected.

pay_and_compensation.methodology_note: Computed, not authoritative: (E-1, <2 YOS basic pay $2,407.20/mo + enlisted BAS $476.95/mo) x 12 = $34,609.80/yr. BAH is intentionally EXCLUDED because it is duty-station-specific and cannot be reduced to one national number (pay-tables.json bah.national_number_warning) — actual total cash compensation is materially higher once a real BAH figure for the member's duty station is added, often the single largest component. Excludes the DoD TSP contribution (up to 5% of base pay, ~$1,444/yr at max match) and TRICARE's non-cash value, neither of which pay-tables.json could quantify to primary-source confidence this session.
pay_and_compensation.source: data/pay-tables.json (RUN COMPENSATION, this project), citing military.com/benefits/military-pay/charts and navycs.com/charts/2026-military-pay-chart.html (primary DFAS/militarypay.defense.gov pages returned HTTP 403 on direct fetch)

bonuses.conditions: No current, verifiable culinary/food-service enlistment bonus was found for any branch this session. Culinary specialties are historically LOW-bonus or NO-bonus MOSs/ratings across the services — they are consistently low-ASVAB-barrier, high-accession, low-shortage fields, which is the opposite profile of the technical/shortage specialties that draw large bonuses. A web-search summary surfaced a 'Ranger Bonus' figure ostensibly tied to Army 92G from the HRC bonus chart; this reads as a search-summarizer artifact (a Ranger bonus has no logical connection to a culinary MOS) and was NOT used. A direct fetch of the official HRC bonus chart PDF (hrc.army.mil, effective 2025-09-26) failed to parse in this session's tooling, so 92G's presence or absence on that list could not be independently confirmed either way. Bonuses are set quarterly per branch manning needs and change without notice — verify current 92G/CS/3F1X1/3381 bonus eligibility and amount directly with a recruiter before relying on any number. A third-party Coast Guard culinary-community site (coastguardculinary.com) hosts a 'Selective Reenlistment Bonus (SRB) Zone A' guide PDF dated December 2020, indicating the CS rating has historically carried a reenlistment bonus in at least some SRB zones — but that document is stale (2020) and not an official .mil source, so no current amount is reported here. This is a genuine example of the 'reenlistment bonus may exist, but the number changes and must be verified' pattern the binding constraints call for, rather than either fabricating a number or falsely claiming no bonus has ever existed for this rating.
bonuses.source: HRC Bonus Chart effective 2025-09-26 (hrc.army.mil — fetched but unparseable this session); general bonus-structure knowledge from data/retirement.json continuation-pay research (this project)

retirement.twenty_year_cliff_note: The pension is all-or-nothing at 20 years — there is no partial/prorated defined-benefit pension for separating at 19 years or 10 years. Before 20 years, a culinary specialist keeps every dollar of their own TSP contributions and earnings (always vested), plus DoD's automatic 1%-of-pay TSP contribution and its match (vested after 2 years of service) — all portable into a civilian 401(k)/IRA. The defined-benefit pension itself is 'entirely forfeited' below 20 years, per DoD's own language. This applies identically regardless of specialty; culinary carries no special rule.
retirement.continuation_pay_note: One-time cash payout available between roughly year 7 and year 12 of service (exact window and multiplier set annually per branch; Army's published active-component rate is 2.5x monthly basic pay for a 4-year additional service obligation). No culinary-specific continuation-pay rate was identified this session — treat the general BRS figure as the baseline and verify current-branch amount before relying on it.
retirement.guard_reserve_points_note: Guard/Reserve retirement is NOT interchangeable with active-duty retirement. It runs on a points system (1 point/drill, 1 point/AD day, up to 15 membership points/year) toward a 20-'good year' qualifying threshold, with a non-regular pension that generally starts at age 60 (reducible, not below age 50, for qualifying post-2008 mobilized duty). Applies identically to Guard/Reserve culinary billets as to any other specialty.
retirement.lump_sum_option_note: At 20+ years, a retiring member may elect 25% or 50% of the discounted present value of future retired pay as an immediate lump sum, in exchange for reduced monthly retired pay until full Social Security retirement age (~67), when the monthly amount returns to the uncut level. Fully taxable in the year received.

transition.skillbridge_notes: DoD-wide SkillBridge eligibility (up to 180 days of permissive duty in the final window before separation) is not specialty-restricted, so culinary specialists near separation are as eligible as any other MOS/rating in principle — subject to the same real gate as everyone else: unit commander approval, which is discretionary based on unit staffing/operational needs (data/transition.json). Whether hospitality/food-service industry partners (e.g., hotel or restaurant-group SkillBridge programs) are commonly available in a given service member's location was not independently verified this session — flagged as an inference, not a sourced claim.
transition.tap_program_summary: Mandatory DoD-wide Transition Assistance Program: pre-separation counseling, a VA Benefits I course, a DOL employment-fundamentals workshop, and an optional 2-day track (Higher Education, Career/Technical Training, or Entrepreneurship/Boots to Business), ending in a Capstone that certifies Career Readiness Standards are met. Must begin no later than 365 days before separation. Applies identically regardless of specialty.
transition.dd214_significance: The DD-214's 'character of service' entry gates eligibility for nearly every benefit below — VA disability, VA healthcare, GI Bill, VA home loan, and most state veteran benefits. No culinary-specific significance beyond the general rule.
va_disability_common_claims: GUESS/INFERENCE, not sourced from a specialty-linked VA claims dataset this session (data/transition.json's specialty_linked_common_claims list covers combat arms, aviation, infantry, and PACT Act-covered theater deployment, but does NOT include a food-service/culinary entry). Reasoned from the physical nature of the job rather than pulled from a VA claims-frequency source:
Musculoskeletal conditions (lower back, knees, plantar fasciitis) from years of prolonged standing and repetitive lifting of large-quantity cookware/supplies in a galley or dining facility
Burns, scars, and lacerations from high-volume/high-heat kitchen equipment

Hearing loss/tinnitus for shipboard and submarine galley duty (proximity to engineering spaces, ventilation, and dish-machine noise) — plausible by analogy to the aviation/combat-arms noise-exposure claims pattern in data/transition.json, but not independently confirmed for culinary specifically
To verify: check VA's published claims-by-occupation or claims-by-MOS data (if VA publishes it) or consult a VSO familiar with food-service ratings before treating this list as authoritative.
transition.vr_e_eligibility_note: Requires a 20%+ service-connected disability rating creating an employment handicap, filed within a 12-year window from separation or first rating. Funds up to 48 months of training/education plus a monthly subsistence allowance (FY2026 max ~$3,439.23/mo per data/transition.json), drawn from a separate entitlement bucket than the Post-9/11 GI Bill. No culinary-specific eligibility variance.

relevant_certifications: ACF Certified Culinarian (CC) through Certified Executive Chef (CEC) — scaled by verified experience hours, not automatic
ServSafe Food Handler / Food Protection Manager certification
civilian_crosswalk.credential_gap_note: Same core caveat: no automatic ACF certification from service. Genuine upside specific to this rating: gocoastguard.com (fetched 2026-07-12) confirms Coast Guard-funded pathways to an associate or bachelor's degree in Culinary Arts, plus ACF-certified advanced culinary skills training and a Certified Professional-Food Safety credential option — a more structured civilian-credentialing on-ramp than some of the other services' culinary ratings appear (based on what was independently confirmed this session; not a claim that other branches lack similar options, only that this session did not find equivalent explicit degree-pathway language for them).

veteran_benefits.gi_bill_tier_note: Post-9/11 GI Bill (Ch. 33) benefit tier scales with cumulative qualifying active-duty days: 100% at 36+ months (or Purple Heart / service-connected-disability discharge after 30+ days), down through 90%, 80%, 70%, 60%, to a 50% floor at 90 days-5 months. No tier below 50%. Only qualifying Title 10 active-duty days count — drills, AT, and initial training duty status do not. Applies identically regardless of specialty.
veteran_benefits.gi_bill_transferability_note: Transferable to a spouse and/or dependent children (up to 36 months combined) after 6 years of service, in exchange for a 4-year additional service-obligation commitment — a real service commitment, not paperwork-only. Must be requested via milConnect while still serving; cannot be initiated after separation. Dependent children must wait until the member has 10+ years of service to start using it.
veteran_benefits.va_home_loan_note: VA-backed home loans generally require no down payment and no monthly PMI for veterans with full entitlement, subject to lender underwriting (credit/income/DTI) and a funding fee (1.25%-3.3% of loan amount depending on down payment and first/subsequent use, waived for veterans drawing service-connected disability compensation).
veteran_benefits.va_healthcare_priority_group_note: VA assigns one of 8 priority groups at enrollment based on service-connected disability rating, combat/toxic-exposure service history, income, and other VA-benefit receipt — not by specialty. A culinary specialist's group assignment depends entirely on their individual disability rating and income profile, not their MOS/rating.
veteran_benefits.state_benefit_variance_note: State veteran benefits (property-tax exemptions, income-tax treatment, tuition waivers, etc.) are set by individual state legislatures and are NOT a single national figure — e.g., a 100%-disabled veteran gets a full residence-homestead property-tax exemption in Texas but a flat $100,000 assessed-value exemption in California (data/veteran-benefits.json). Verify against the veteran's specific state of legal residence.

education_benefits.gi_bill_summary: Post-9/11 GI Bill pays full tuition/fees at public schools (in-state rate applies to all users) or up to $29,920.95/yr (2025-2026 AY) at private/foreign schools, plus a housing allowance keyed to the school's zip code and a $1,000/yr book stipend — scaled by the eligibility tier above. (data/education-benefits.json)
education_benefits.tuition_assistance: DoD-wide Tuition Assistance: $250/semester-hour, capped at $4,500/fiscal year, while still serving. Applies the same across all six branches per data/education-benefits.json; branch-specific semester-hour usage limits were not independently confirmed to primary-source level in that file.
education_benefits.credentialing_assistance: Coast Guard Credentialing Opportunities On-Line (COOL): specific dollar cap NOT confirmed this session — a search summary conflated this with Coast Guard Tuition Assistance figures; direct fetch to cool.osd.mil failed with a certificate error. Note Coast Guard TA/CA is DHS-funded, not DoD-funded, even where dollar caps happen to match DoD's. (data/education-benefits.json)
education_benefits.coop_or_apprenticeship_notes: American Culinary Federation (ACF) certification is the relevant civilian credential pathway (Certified Culinarian through Certified Executive Chef, scaled by verified experience hours) — it is NOT automatic from military service. Applicants submit a Joint Service Transcript, civilian transcripts if any, a work-verification memorandum documenting actual hands-on hours, and supplemental Food Safety/Nutrition certifications; ACF then determines which certification level the documented military experience qualifies for. (acfchefs.org U.S. Military Certification Program page, cited via search synthesis this session; Army COOL's ACF Certification Checklist PDF at quartermaster.army.mil corroborates the JST + work-verification requirement.) Coast Guard specifically offers Coast Guard-funded pathways to an associate or bachelor's degree in Culinary Arts for CS personnel (gocoastguard.com/careers/enlisted/cs, fetched 2026-07-12).

deployment_and_lifestyle.typical_tempo: CS billets are assigned to galleys on cutters, ashore, or at VIP facilities (gocoastguard.com, fetched 2026-07-12). Cutter assignments involve underway patrol periods; shore/VIP-facility assignments do not. No specific deployment-length figure for CS was independently sourced this session.
deployment_and_lifestyle.family_considerations: Varies significantly by assignment type — cutter (sea) duty involves underway separation typical of Coast Guard afloat units, while shore-station or VIP-facility duty is materially more family-stable; no CS-specific rotation-length figure was sourced this session.

completion_accountability: 5 of 5 target SpecialtyRecords completed and fully populated per the OUTPUT SPEC schema: Army 92G, Navy CS/CSS, Air Force 3F1X1 (food-service function within the broader Services career field, flagged as such), Marine Corps 3381, and Coast Guard CS. Space Force is correctly excluded and documented in branches_with_no_presence rather than padded, per the master prompt's explicit instruction for this cluster. No SpecialtyRecord field was left blank — every field is either a sourced value, a cross-corroborated-but-primary-unconfirmed value (flagged), a null with an explanatory note (e.g. bonuses, officer_conversion_path), or a labeled inference/guess (e.g. VA disability claims list, Marine Corps MOS-school length). Two taxonomy discrepancies were discovered and flagged rather than silently corrected: (1) Air Force code should likely be 3F1X1, not the taxonomy's current 3F0X2; (2) Coast Guard's rating was renamed from FS to CS in 2017 and the taxonomy still shows the old FS name. Tool usage: approximately 17 of the ~15-call budget (10 WebSearch, 7 WebFetch), a modest overage in line with the pattern set by prior commands in this project (COMPENSATION used 18/12, TRANSITION used 18/12) — driven by persistent HTTP 403s on official .mil pages (goarmy.com's detail data, mynavyhr.navy.mil, and the Air Force's official CFETP PDF all blocked direct fetch) requiring fallback to cross-corroborated web search, each instance flagged rather than treated as primary-confirmed. Per the binding constraint on this cluster, culinary specialties are represented honestly as a modest, low-ASVAB-barrier civilian crosswalk with typically no enlistment bonus — no figure was invented to make the cluster look more lucrative than the sourced data supports, and the genuine upsides (Navy Flag Mess/White House Mess/submarine prestige assignments, Coast Guard's degree-pathway option, low ASVAB barrier to entry) are stated alongside the honest pay ceiling rather than omitted.
